title: How New Parties Are Built in Malaysia
subtitle: A Field Guide to the Launch-and-Pivot Playbook
stageScoresNote: Stage 1 (Primary Analysis) 80; Stage 2 (Bias Audit) 98 (bias_score 1.5); Stage 3 (Fact Verification) trajectory across five reviewer passes: v1 60 → v2 82 → v3 87 → v4 84 → v5 90 READY_TO_PUBLISH with zero cardinal sin flags; Stage 4 (Alt-Framing) 65 with remaining completeness gaps deferred to future dossiers in the Malaysia Political Mechanics series; Stage 5 (Contrarian) 52 with cowardice flags addressed in synthesis; Stage 6 (Synthesis) 90 with all reviewer findings across all four stages resolved.
finalScoreNote: Weighted average across all six editorial review stages. Cleared the 85 factual-accuracy publication threshold on the fifth review pass with zero remaining cardinal sin flags. Completeness and contrarian scores deliberately reflect deferred items that the future dossiers in the series will address.
citeAs: The Fourth Angle (2026). How New Parties Are Built in Malaysia: A Field Guide to the Launch-and-Pivot Playbook. Dossier D001. https://thefourthangle.pages.dev/dossier/D001

sections.title: How New Parties Are Built in Malaysia
sections.subtitle: A Field Guide to the Launch-and-Pivot Playbook
whatYoullLearn: Why a new party appears in Malaysia every election cycle, and what the launcher is actually trying to win
The six structural conditions that make the move repeatable here and uneconomic almost everywhere else
Sabah's role as the laboratory and Sarawak's role as the structural counter-example
The exact text of the 2022 anti-hopping law and the loophole hiding inside it
How a voter should read a launch without falling for either cynicism or naïveté

sections.title: The dossier in 7 lines
bullets: A new Malaysian party in the 12 months before an election is almost never trying to win the country. It is trying to win the negotiation the morning after the count.
The prize is pivotal-player status: enough seats to be the necessary coalition partner in a fragmented chamber.

Six structural conditions make Malaysia uniquely fertile soil: low registration bar, ethnic cleavages, portable patronage, post-electoral coalition formation, the 2022 anti-hopping law's party-level loophole, and personalist politics.

Sabah is the laboratory; the state has had eight ruling configurations in forty years. Sarawak's GPS is the stable counter-example, fiscally anchored by petroleum sales-tax revenue of roughly RM3 to RM4 billion a year, in a state assembly where opposition representation has shrunk from six members to two of eighty-two as of April 2024.

Most new parties fail. The fastest office-seeking exceptions (Bersatu in 2016 and GRS in 2020) share three ingredients at launch: sitting MPs in the launch party, executive office on offer in a friendly coalition, and a pre-negotiated coalition slot. PKR is a different kind of exception: a Reformasi durability vehicle that took multiple election cycles to convert movement politics into institutional power.
Other democracies block this move with thresholds, public-funding rules, ballot-access barriers, and party-property law. Malaysia has chosen none of these defences.

A voter is not the audience for the launch speech. A voter is the resource the launcher needs to be in a room the voter cannot see. Vote by judging who will be in that room, and push, where you can, for that room to be opened to outside scrutiny.

sections.title: What pivotal-player status actually means
sections.body: In a chamber where no single bloc commands a majority, the small party with the right number of seats holds bargaining power wildly out of proportion to its seat share. The formal name is the Shapley-Shubik power index, and its arithmetic is unforgiving. A 30-seat bloc in an 80-80-30 chamber holds about 16 per cent of the seats but exactly one-third of the Shapley-Shubik bargaining power. The chamber has three blocs and six possible orderings of them; the 30-seat bloc is the pivot that pushes the running total past the 96-seat majority quota in two of those six orderings. Two-sixths is one-third. The structural prize every successful new Malaysian party has aimed at is in that disproportion: not to govern, but to be necessary. Once you see the chamber this way, the launches stop looking irrational. They look like a rational play by people who have done the math.

sections.title: What the base rates actually say
sections.body: The available examples suggest two distinct exception patterns. Across new federal parties launched in Malaysia between 1988 and 2024, the typical first-contest result is a single-digit fraction of the seats they choose to contest. Many do not survive five years; they are deregistered, dormant, or absorbed. The fast office-seeking exceptions (Bersatu in 2016 the clearest case at federal level, GRS in 2020 at state level) reach government within two elections of formation by combining the three structural ingredients above: sitting MPs in the launch party, an executive office on offer in a friendly coalition, and a pre-negotiated slot at the table. A separate exception model is the durability launch, where a movement vehicle survives early electoral marginality and converts to power over multiple cycles: PKR after 1999 is the canonical case, winning five seats at first contest and reaching federal government only nineteen years later through coalition. Personality-only vehicles (IMP in 1951, Mahathir's Pejuang and the GTA umbrella in 2022, MUDA at federal level) have rarely converted to office by either route. The empirical lesson, even on the cases the dossier examines closely, is not that any single move always works. It is that the fast pivot works under three specific conditions, the durability route works under a different set, and personality alone works under neither.
citations: EC results 1988–2024
Bridget Welsh GE-cycle voter analyses
Wong Chin Huat / Penang Institute electoral-system commentary

sections.title: Every major launch, 1951 to 2024
sections.intro: The chain matters because each iteration tightens the playbook. The 2026–27 cycle is being read by every operator through this seventy-year arc.

events.label: IMP
events.outcome: Failed: multi-racial vehicle ahead of the cleavage

events.label: DAP and Gerakan
events.founder: DAP formed 1965, registered March 1966; Gerakan founded March 1968
events.outcome: Both broke through electorally in the 1969 general election; broke MCA monopoly on Chinese-language politics

events.label: KeADILan
events.outcome: 5 seats at first contest; durable spine of PKR

events.label: Amanah
events.outcome: Durable junior PH partner since GE14

events.label: Bersatu
events.outcome: Pivotal: flipped federal power in GE14

events.label: Pejuang
events.note: Founded by announcement August 2020; Registrar of Societies approval July 2021.

events.label: MUDA
events.note: Founded by announcement September 2020; Registrar of Societies approval December 2021.

events.label: GTA umbrella
events.outcome: Wiped out in GE15; deposits lost

sections.title: Bersatu 2016: the textbook successful launch
sections.body:
The Malaysian United Indigenous Party (Parti Pribumi Bersatu Malaysia) was approved by the Registrar of Societies in September 2016, and within twenty months had toppled the Barisan Nasional government that had run Malaysia since independence. The launchers were Mahathir Mohamad and Muhyiddin Yassin, two former senior UMNO leaders with portable patronage, decades of branch-network credibility in the rural Malay heartland, and immediate sitting-MP defections at formation. The grievance was 1MDB: a generational anti-corruption frame that UMNO structurally could not respond to without attacking its own leadership. The coalition slot was pre-negotiated with PKR, DAP, and Amanah inside the Pakatan Harapan umbrella.

The three ingredients (defectors with seats, a grievance with civic infrastructure, a pre-negotiated coalition slot) were all present simultaneously. That alignment is rare. When it happens, the rest of the system bends.

The structural ingredients explain why the move was possible. Personal incentives (cabinet posts, control of state-linked corporations, ministerial portfolios distributed to the defectors) explain why the move was attractive to each individual operator. The same logic ran through the 2020 Sheraton Move that ejected Mahathir from government: senior posts, GLC access, and titles distributed within weeks of the defection.
citations: ROS gazette 2016–2017
EC GE14 results
Bersih GE14 monitoring report
ResearchGate: Malaysia in 2020: Fragile Coalitional Politics and Democratic Regression

sections.title: Pejuang 2020: the textbook failed launch
sections.body:
Three years after Bersatu's triumph, Mahathir announced a new party in August 2020 after the Sheraton Move ejected him from government; Parti Pejuang Tanah Air was formally registered in July 2021. In GE15 in November 2022, Mahathir's Gerakan Tanah Air (GTA) umbrella, with Pejuang as principal vehicle, announced 121 parliamentary candidates and contested seats in nearly every state. The bloc won zero seats; Mahathir himself lost his Langkawi seat, and a large share of GTA candidates including the founder forfeited the RM10,000 deposit by polling under one-eighth of the valid votes cast. The contrast with Bersatu is a controlled experiment for the three-ingredient framework. Same launcher, same operational skill, different ingredients, opposite result. Sitting MPs had already chosen their vehicles (PN, BN, PH). The grievance, anti-Bersatu and anti-Anwar, was personal, not generational. No coalition slot had been pre-negotiated; the GTA umbrella was a thin afterthought. The base rates do not bend for legendary founders. They bend for the three ingredients.

Semangat 46 is the awkward case the framework has to address. Tengku Razaleigh broke from UMNO in 1988 with the first ingredient (defectors with seats) and a clean leadership-legitimacy grievance, but no pre-negotiated coalition slot strong enough to clear the FPTP arithmetic. The party still won 8 seats in 1990 and forced UMNO into one of its more contested campaigns of the era, before being reabsorbed in 1996. Two ingredients out of three produced real but bounded success: enough to extract concessions and embarrass the centre, not enough to govern. The framework predicts the ceiling, not just the floor. That asymmetry is the part the launch-cycle literature tends to flatten.
citations: Malaysiakini coverage of Pejuang announcement August 2020
Astro Awani: Pejuang registered July 2021
Bernama GE15 candidate counts
Election Offences Act 1954 §14 on deposit forfeiture threshold

sections.body: The launch is not about the voter. The voter is the input the launcher needs to reach a table the voter is not at. The negotiation that decides who governs Malaysia happens in the seventy-two hours after the count, between coalition leaders, royal advisors, and party whips. The voter's role is upstream of that room. A vote for a small new party is not a purchase of policy. It is a loan of bargaining chips to be spent later, by leadership the voter must trust on judgement.

sections.title: Lipset–Rokkan cleavages, applied locally
sections.body: In 1967, two political sociologists named Seymour Martin Lipset and Stein Rokkan argued that party systems freeze around the major social cleavages of a country at the moment of mass enfranchisement: class against capital, region against centre, religion against secular state. Their insight, transported to Malaysia, predicts exactly the pattern we see. Malaysian voters are sorted along multiple axes that do not collapse into a single left-right line: Malay-Chinese-Indian, Bumiputera-Bornean-non-Malay, urban-rural, secular-Islamist, peninsular-territorial. Each axis is a potential niche for a new entrant. This is why the same first-past-the-post electoral system that produces two-party politics in the United Kingdom produces a fragmented multi-party landscape here. The mechanical incentive runs one way; the sociology runs another, and wins.
citations: Lipset & Rokkan 1967
Wong Chin Huat on Malaysian cleavages

sections.title: The six structural conditions that make this Malaysian
sections.body: First, the registration bar is administrative, not constitutional. Parties register under the Societies Act 1966, the same statute that governs sports clubs. Second, ethnic and territorial cleavages create defensible niches. Third, patronage is portable, not ideological. A district chief who switches parties brings the branch chairs and contractor networks with him. Fourth, coalitions in Malaysia are increasingly negotiated after the count, not before. Fifth, the 2022 anti-hopping law closed individual defection but not party-level coalition exits. Sixth, voters in many seats vote the candidate, not the manifesto, which lowers ideological friction for any re-branding. Remove any two of these and the launch playbook becomes uneconomic. Together they create a market opening that rational operators have learned to exploit.
citations: Societies Act 1966
Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 (Act A1663) inserting Article 49A
Edmund Terence Gomez on patronage networks in Malaysian politics
Bridget Welsh on candidate-centric voting in Malaysian constituencies

sections.title: One vote is not one vote
sections.body: The single most decisive, least-discussed feature of Malaysian elections is malapportionment. The original 1957 Federal Constitution capped voter-number deviation between constituencies at fifteen per cent. A 1962 amendment repealed that rule and moved apportionment principles into the Thirteenth Schedule. A 1973 amendment removed the quantified national deviation cap entirely from the current text. Today, by the Election Commission's own published voter roll for the GE15 (PRU15) delineation cycle, the largest parliamentary constituency contains roughly ten to eleven times as many voters as the smallest: Bangi in suburban Selangor at about 303,000 registered voters, Igan in rural Sarawak at about 28,000. A vote cast in Bangi carries roughly one-tenth the weight of a vote cast in Igan. This is not a slogan. This is arithmetic on the EC's own roll. The UNDI18 reform of 2021 added roughly 5.8 million new voters through automatic registration, and the post-UNDI18 roll shows the disparity remains severe rather than narrowing. Strategic consequence: any new party building an urban-peninsular base must out-perform its target seat share in raw votes by a substantial margin. Bornean and rural-Malay seats are the cheapest votes per seat won; this is why every coalition that has formed federal government since 2008 has been built around them.
citations: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Article 113
Constitution (Amendment) Acts 1962 and 1973
Election Commission PRU15 voter-roll statistics by parliamentary constituency
Wong Chin Huat malapportionment papers, Penang Institute / Bersih

sections.title: The anti-hopping law and the loophole hiding inside it
sections.body: The Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 (Act A1663) inserted Article 49A into the Federal Constitution, triggering a casual vacancy whenever an MP resigns from the party they were elected under, or otherwise ceases to be a member of that party. It was sold as the end of frog politics, and on that narrow ground it has worked. Two loopholes survive inside the text. First, expulsion: under Article 49A(2)(c), an MP who is expelled by the party (rather than resigning) does not lose the seat. Second, party-level: the law restricts the individual, not the party as a corporate body. A whole party can vote internally to leave one coalition and join another, and its MPs keep their seats. A party that is dissolved or deregistered also produces no casual vacancy. The structural incentive to form a vehicle in advance of a coalition move has therefore gone up, not down. Malaysia made individual resignation illegal, left expulsion as an unintended escape route, and made buying the whole pond entirely legal.
citations: Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 (Act A1663)
Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Article 49A(1) and Article 49A(2)(c)
Malaysian Bar Council, ILRC Submission on Election Commission and Article 49A commentary

sections.source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Article 49A, inserted by the Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 (Act A1663). Royal Assent 31 August 2022; published in the Gazette 6 September 2022
sections.body: Article 49A provides that a Member of the House of Representatives ceases to be a Member, with the seat declared vacant, when, having been elected as a member of a political party, the Member resigns from or ceases to be a member of that party. Article 49A(2)(c) explicitly excludes expulsion from this trigger: a Member expelled by the party does not lose the seat.
sections.commentary: Read the structure carefully. The law binds the Member. It does not bind the party. And it does not bind expulsion. A party can dissolve, re-form, or vote en bloc to switch coalitions, and its MPs keep their seats because no individual has resigned. A party can also expel a Member without that Member losing the seat. The resignation route is closed, the expulsion route was deliberately left open. This is not an obscure interpretation. It is what the words say.

sections.title: Eight ruling configurations in forty years
sections.body: Sabah is where the launch-and-pivot play has been refined into an art form. Since the late 1970s the state has cycled through Berjaya, USNO, PBS, BN-controlled, Musa-era BN, Warisan, and GRS as ruling configurations, with multiple component-party recombinations in between. The three structural reasons are visible. First, the federal-territorial cleavage anchored in the Twenty Points and MA63 gives Sabah a permanent grievance frame no peninsular party can fully own. Second, the Bumiputera-non-Malay ethnic composition (Kadazan-Dusun-Murut, Sino-Kadazan, Sulu, Bajau) does not map onto peninsular categories, so niche space is large. Third, the dependence on Putrajaya for federal allocations combined with resentment of Putrajaya produces stable demand for vehicles that can negotiate with and signal against the centre simultaneously. Every new Sabah party is sold as that vehicle. A fourth structural factor sits in the background and changes how the first three should be read. The 2014 Royal Commission of Inquiry on illegal immigrants in Sabah, established under royal warrant, documented illegal or irregular issuance of Malaysian identity documents and evidence linking some recipients to the electoral roll. As of late 2021, opposition MPs and civil-society critics reported that the RCI's core recommendations remained largely unimplemented; no comprehensive government implementation status report has since been published. Whatever the political weather above the surface, the electoral soil itself has been worked.
citations: State Government of Sabah historical record
James Chin Sabah political economy papers
Royal Commission of Inquiry on Illegal Immigrants in Sabah, Final Report 2014
The Vibes Nov 2021 on RCI non-implementation

sections.title: GRS 2020: the case study
sections.body: Gabungan Rakyat Sabah was formed in the weeks immediately before the September 2020 Sabah state election as a loose electoral umbrella of Perikatan Nasional, Barisan Nasional and Parti Bersatu Sabah. Its product was the umbrella itself: a brand that let component parties contest without splitting the anti-Warisan vote under first-past-the-post. It won. The later, formally registered Gabungan Rakyat Sabah in 2022 comprised Bersatu, PBS, STAR and SAPP (without BN and PAS), distinct from the 2020 electoral pact. The lesson taken across the corridor was that the brand is the product, and speed beats depth. In a state-level election, with a few weeks of preparation, a defector-heavy umbrella can defeat a sitting government if the alternative is a fragmented opposition vote. The 2025–26 Sabah cycle will be read against this template, by every operator considering a launch.
citations: MalayMail Sabah election 2020 results coverage
Bernama on 2022 GRS formal registration composition

sections.title: Sarawak's structural counter-example
sections.body: If Sabah is the laboratory of volatility, Sarawak is the laboratory of stability. The Gabungan Parti Sarawak coalition has held the state without major fragmentation since exiting Barisan Nasional in 2018, while every other peninsular and Sabah configuration has churned. The reason is fiscal, and political. In 2018 the Sarawak state government imposed a five per cent State Sales Tax on petroleum products produced or imported within the state. Audited collections reported in successive state budget speeches ranged from RM2.74 billion in 2020 to RM4.23 billion in 2022, with cumulative collection over 2019 to 2023 in the range of RM15 to 17 billion. This revenue is largely state-controlled. It does not require federal goodwill to collect; it does not depend on coalition arithmetic in Putrajaya. With that fiscal floor in place, GPS does not need to fragment to extract resources from the centre. The political environment in Sarawak is harder to read with confidence: DAP and other opposition representatives have publicly stated that opposition-held constituencies receive lower state development allocations, and opposition representation in the eighty-two-seat state assembly has shrunk from six members post-2021 to two as of April 2024, after the dissolution of Parti Sarawak Bersatu and the realignment of former PSB assemblymen. Sabah does not have an equivalent fiscal lever; neither does any peninsular state. Stability in Sarawak is partly fiscal and partly political.
citations: Sarawak Sales Tax (Amendment) Ordinance 2018
Sarawak state budget statements 2019–2024
FreeMalaysiaToday July 2025 on opposition fund denial
Faisal S. Hazis on Sarawak political economy

sections.title: The three things a launch is probably doing
sections.body: When a new party appears in the twelve months before a vote, the prior probability (given the base rates above) is that it is doing one of three things. The pivot play: a defector with sitting seats is positioning to be the kingmaker in a hung parliament. Tell: existing MPs join early, coalition slot is hinted before manifesto. The grievance vehicle: a real generational dissatisfaction has no party that owns it, and the launch is the channel. Tell: civil-society figures involved, manifesto precedes machine, ideology coherent. The vanity option: a personality, often older, registering a vehicle to retain relevance or future-option a return. Tell: founder-centric naming, thin bench, no clear coalition slot, no defining issue. The first two can be voted on rationally. The third is almost always a wasted vote.

sections.correction: The strongest version of this position is the elite-continuity argument. Loh Chee Seng wrote in Aliran on 27 January 2026 that 'power circulates within overlapping cabals comprising the political elites, party-linked business groups, government-linked companies and bureaucratic gatekeepers' (https://m.aliran.com/thinking-allowed-online/beyond-ethno-populism-how-elite-groups-manipulate-identity-politics-in-malaysia). The framework partially agrees. New-party launchers are drawn from a narrow pool of former cabinet ministers, senior UMNO, PKR, DAP and PAS defectors, and patronage-rooted business networks. The class of launchers is durable. The outcomes the class delivers are not. Bersatu 2016 carried a generational anti-corruption frame to federal power; PKR after 1999 institutionalised Reformasi politics over two decades; GTA 2022 forfeited deposits across nearly every contested seat. The discriminating question is not whether the launchers come from the same pool. It is which combination of structural ingredients each vehicle assembles before contest day.

sections.claim: This one is the reformer who will fix it
sections.correction: The base rate of reformer-messiah launches actually delivering the reform they sold is low. Of the named-reformer launches in Malaysia since 1990, the ones that converted to durable institutional reform without first capturing executive office and re-allocating patronage are vanishingly few. The discriminating posture is structural scepticism: trust the answers to the five structural questions below more than the launch speech.

sections.title: Five structural questions to ask before voting
sections.body: Forget the speeches. The answers to these five questions are public information and they discriminate. One: who is already inside? Sitting MPs or assemblypersons at launch indicate a pivot or grievance play. None of them indicates a vanity option. Two: who is funding it? Campaign-finance disclosure is weak, but rally logistics and advertising spend leak the answer. A new party with national visibility and no obvious patron has a hidden one. Three: what is the coalition slot? Has the leadership named who it would work with? A party that refuses to answer is reserving the right to sell the answer after you vote. Four: what job does this party do that no existing party does? If the honest answer is the same job UMNO or PKR or PAS does, just with this person leading, the product is a personality, not a service. Five: where would they be in five years if this election goes badly? Real grievance vehicles survive bad results. Vanity vehicles disappear.

sections.title: The pivotal-player problem from the voter's chair
sections.body: The most uncomfortable insight in this dossier is this: when a voter casts for a small new party expecting it to deliver policy X, what the voter usually delivers is bargaining power, not policy. The Shapley-value logic that makes the launch rational from the operator's side cuts the same way from the voter's side. A vote for a pivot party is a vote of confidence in the negotiating judgement of its leadership, not a purchase of the policies on the manifesto. That is a coherent thing to vote for. But it is a different vote from the one most launch campaigns describe. If you would not trust this leadership to negotiate on your behalf in a closed room you cannot enter, voting for them on policy grounds is a category error.

A new-party launch should shift your opinion not on the launcher's claims, but on the system that produced the launch. The honest question is not whether this person is trustworthy. It is what failure of the existing parties created the opening this launch is exploiting. That question puts you back in command. The launcher is responding to a market gap; your job is to see the gap clearly. Then ask the simpler test before voting: if this new party did not exist, where would my vote go and why? If the answer is to the same coalition, the launch has not changed the math. If the answer is I would not vote, the launch is doing real work. You are not the audience for the launch speech. You are the resource the launcher needs to be in a room you cannot see, where the deal you will live under is decided. Vote accordingly, by judging who will be in that room, not by judging the speech. One honesty test before you close the dossier. The framework above assumes the voter has a meaningful choice about which room to send a representative to. For many Malaysian voters (the rural Felda settler whose JKKK head determines welfare access, the plantation worker whose contractor depends on the ruling coalition's allocations, the village kepala kampung whose appointment is the government's gift), the calculation is not 'which leader do I trust to negotiate', it is 'which vote is safe given who already controls my livelihood'. For those voters, the pivot-player framework is theoretical until campaign finance, voter-roll integrity, and patronage allocation are themselves opened to outside scrutiny. The deepest reform is not which new party launches next. It is whether the room becomes one that outside witnesses can audit.

sections.title: If you want to go deeper

items.title: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Articles 40, 49A, 113, 121, 150
items.note: Read the text directly; it is shorter and clearer than the commentary.

items.title: Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022
items.note: The anti-hopping insertion. Read with Article 49A side by side.

items.title: Edmund Terence Gomez and K.S. Jomo, Malaysia's Political Economy: Politics, Patronage and Profits (2nd edition, Cambridge University Press, 1999)
items.note: The patronage-and-business spine of modern Malaysian politics.

items.title: Meredith L. Weiss, Protest and Possibilities: Civil Society and Coalitions for Political Change in Malaysia (Stanford University Press, 2006)
items.note: On civil society and coalition formation; the comparative chapter on illiberal-democracy reform pathways is the most relevant for this dossier.

items.title: James Chin, 'Exporting the BN/UMNO Model: Politics in Sabah and Sarawak', in Routledge Handbook of Contemporary Malaysia (2015), pp. 83–92
items.note: Indispensable for the Bornean half of the story.

items.title: Faisal S. Hazis, Domination and Contestation: Muslim Bumiputera Politics in Sarawak (ISEAS, 2012)
items.note: The deepest single account of Sarawak's Melanau-strongman political economy, 1970–2006.

items.title: Bridget Welsh, 'Saviour Politics and Malaysia's 2018 Electoral Democratic Breakthrough: Rethinking Explanatory Narratives and Implications', Journal of Current Southeast Asian Affairs 37(3), 2018
items.note: The survey-based read of how voters actually decide; updated GE15 ethnic-voting analyses at bridgetwelsh.com.

items.title: Wong Chin Huat, 'Constituency Delimitation and Electoral Authoritarianism in Malaysia', The Round Table 107(1), 2018
items.note: The clearest peer-reviewed accounting of the EC roll's deviation; supplemented by his Penang Institute report on the 2016 EC recommendations.

items.title: Seymour Martin Lipset & Stein Rokkan (1967), Party Systems and Voter Alignments
items.note: The cleavage theory the dossier rests on.

items.title: William Riker (1962), The Theory of Political Coalitions
items.note: The minimum-winning-coalition logic that makes pivot value rational.

items.title: Sona Golder (2006), The Logic of Pre-Electoral Coalition Formation
items.note: Why some coalitions form before the vote and some after.

items.title: Bersih reports on delimitation and electoral reform
items.note: The reform-side reading of the same data.

items.title: Royal Commission of Inquiry on Illegal Immigrants in Sabah, Final Report (2014)
items.note: The documentary spine of any honest analysis of Sabah's electoral arithmetic.

items.title: Cas Mudde and Cristóbal Rovira Kaltwasser, Populism: A Very Short Introduction
items.note: For the populist-launch literature the dossier touches but does not deep-dive.

sections.title: Cite this dossier
sections.format: The Fourth Angle (2026). How New Parties Are Built in Malaysia: A Field Guide to the Launch-and-Pivot Playbook. Dossier D001, Series 1: Malaysia Political Mechanics. Retrieved from https://thefourthangle.pages.dev/dossier/D001

figures.title: Cover illustration
figures.alt: Single continuous white line drawing of figures around a table in a darkened room, with a window showing distant crowds
figures.purpose: Hero image: evokes the negotiation room the voter cannot see
figures.caption: The negotiation room the voter cannot see. Four figures around a table, a window suggesting a distant crowd: the few in the room, the many outside it.

figures.title: Figure 1: The Shapley pivot
figures.alt: Schematic of an 80-80-30 seat chamber showing the 30-seat party as the necessary partner in either majority coalition, with a secondary panel showing the grand-coalition case where the pivot value evaporates
figures.purpose: Anchor for the pivotal-player concept section
figures.caption: The pivotal player in a hung chamber, and the case where the pivot evaporates. The 30-seat bloc holds exactly one-third of the Shapley–Shubik power on about one-sixth of the seats. The exception: when the two large blocs form a grand coalition, the pivot premium vanishes.

figures.title: Figure 2: Every major party launch, 1951–2024
figures.alt: Horizontal timeline with markers for IMP 1951 through GTA 2022, colour-coded by outcome
figures.caption: Major Malaysian federal-or-state party launches, 1951–2024. Filled marker = successful pivot. Outline = partial. Faded = failed.
figures.sourceLine: Source: ROS gazette records, Election Commission results, T4A compilation.

figures.title: Figure 3: The brokerage stack
figures.alt: Vertical layered diagram showing what a defector brings: branch chairs, JKKK village chiefs and mosque committees, Felda ties, contractor networks
figures.caption: What a defector with seats actually delivers. The visible defection is the top layer; the four layers beneath are the assets a new party cannot otherwise acquire in less than a decade.

figures.title: Figure 4: Voters per parliamentary seat, sample of nine
figures.alt: Horizontal bar chart showing voter counts per parliamentary seat, ranging from ~28,000 to ~303,000
figures.caption: Voters per parliamentary constituency, sample of nine from the EC GE15 (2022) roll, post-UNDI18. The largest seat carries roughly 10.7 times the voters of the smallest. The 1957 Federal Constitution capped this deviation at 15 per cent; a 1962 amendment loosened it; a 1973 amendment removed the quantified cap entirely.
figures.sourceLine: Source: Election Commission of Malaysia, PRU15 voter-roll statistics by parliamentary constituency.

figures.title: Figure 5: The anti-hopping loophole
figures.alt: Continuous-line illustration: on the left, a single figure in a suit stands facing a closed door fitted with a padlock, three burgundy strokes flaring around the lock; on the right, an open archway with three figures in suits walking through it together. Captions beneath each scene read INDIVIDUAL ROUTE and COORDINATED PARTY-LEVEL ROUTE.
figures.caption: Article 49A, inserted in 2022, binds individual MPs but not parties.
figures.sourceLine: Source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Article 49A; Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022 (Act A1663).

figures.title: Figure 6: International comparisons
figures.alt: Table comparing Malaysia, Germany, UK, Indonesia, Israel, Italy on entry barriers, threshold, public funding, anti-hop rules
figures.purpose: Standalone comparative reference in Part Two
figures.caption: Comparative entry barriers across six democracies. Malaysia sits in the same bucket as Israel and Italy for ease of new-party formation, and well outside Germany or the United Kingdom. The reform options exist; they have simply not been chosen.
figures.sourceLine: Source: T4A comparative survey of electoral law and party-system rules across six jurisdictions, 2024.

figures.title: Figure 7: Sarawak State Sales Tax revenue, 2019–2024
figures.alt: Vertical bar chart of Sarawak SST revenue per year, RM2.7bn–RM4.2bn range
figures.caption: Sarawak State Sales Tax revenue from petroleum products, 2019–2024, in billions of ringgit. Tax imposed at 5 per cent by the Sarawak Sales Tax (Amendment) Ordinance 2018. Cumulative collection 2019–2023 in the range of RM15–17 billion.
figures.sourceLine: Sources: Sarawak State Budget speeches 2022, 2023, 2024 (Sarawak Premier’s Department); Sarawak Premier’s media statement of 17 September 2020.
